channel.title: Nicotine Science and Policy
channel.description: Daily digest of views, debates and discussions on nicotine science, policy, regulation and advocacy.

item.title: How vaping scare stories are made
item.description: Vaping scare stories often begin with selective framing rather than balanced evidence. When studies are reported without proper context on relative risk, exposure levels or comparisons with smoking, public understanding can quickly become distorted. This matters because exaggerated risk perception may discourage adult smokers from switching to lower-risk alternatives. Better health communication should inform the public accurately, not turn complex evidence into fear-driven headlines. (Published on June 10, 2026bysubstack.com)

item.title: Smoke-Free by 2040: Why the Route Matters as Much as the Destination
item.description: The European Commission aims for a "smoke-free generation" by 2040, focusing on reducing smoking-related diseases. The debate revolves around treating nicotine or combustion as the main issue, with evidence supporting combustion as the primary cause of smoking-related deaths. The Commission's consultation on the Tobacco Products Directive should consider successful quitting experiences with smoke-free alternatives and differentiate between tobacco products' risks appropriately. Sweden's success in reducing smoking rates through lower-risk alternatives serves as a model. A credible consultation should acknowledge this evidence, leading to more effective tobacco control policies in the EU. (Published on June 10, 2026bytobaccoharmreduction.net)

item.title: Why Is It Harder for People in Rural Communities to Quit Smoking?
item.description: Rural communities often face greater barriers to quitting smoking, from limited healthcare access to higher stress, stigma and fewer cessation support options. These challenges make it especially important that tobacco policy reflects real-world inequalities rather than assuming one approach works for everyone. Harm reduction can offer more practical pathways for smokers who may struggle to access or succeed with conventional quitting methods. Reducing smoking-related harm requires policies that meet people where they are, not only where public health systems expect them to be. (Published on June 10, 2026byfiltermag.org)

item.title: A beacon of reason in a ideological war | GFN26 Closing remarks by Konstantinos Farsalinos
item.description: Public health loses credibility when ideology takes priority over evidence, empathy and real-world outcomes. The closing remarks from GFN26 call for a return to harm reduction principles: meeting people where they are, protecting access to safer alternatives and recognising adult autonomy. In nicotine policy, the goal should be reducing smoking-related disease — not punishing people who use nicotine. Evidence-based regulation must defend both public health and the right to make informed choices. (Published on June 10, 2026bygfn.tv)

item.title: FDA defends heightened review requirement for flavored vapes at Fifth Circuit
item.description: The FDA’s stricter review requirements for flavoured vapes are adding to the legal and public health debate over how reduced-risk nicotine products should be regulated. While regulators point to youth protection concerns, critics argue that overly demanding approval standards may limit adult smokers’ access to alternatives that help them move away from cigarettes. The case highlights a central tension in nicotine policy: preventing youth uptake without undermining harm reduction for adults. Regulation should be rigorous, but it should also reflect relative risk, consumer behaviour and the public health cost of keeping cigarettes more accessible than safer alternatives. (Published on June 10, 2026bycourthousenews.com)
